back: A type of AI that can create new content — text, images, audio, video, code — by learning patterns from training data. Unlike traditional AI that classifies or predicts, gen AI produces novel artifacts.

front: What is a Large Language Model (LLM)?
back: A deep learning model trained on massive text corpora to understand and generate human language. LLMs learn statistical patterns to predict the next token. Examples: Gemini, GPT-4, Llama.

The neural network architecture (introduced 2017) that powers most modern LLMs. Key innovation: self-attention mechanism, which allows the model to consider relationships between all tokens in the sequence simultaneously, enabling parallel processing.

front: What are the phases of the ML lifecycle?
back: 1) Problem definition, 2) Data collection & preparation, 3) Feature engineering, 4) Model training, 5) Evaluation & validation, 6) Deployment, 7) Monitoring & maintenance.

When a model learns training data too well (including noise), performing well on training data but poorly on unseen data. Solutions: regularization, dropout, more data, simpler model, cross-validation.

front: Supervised vs Unsupervised vs Reinforcement Learning
back: Supervised: labeled data, learns input→output mapping (classification, regression). Unsupervised: unlabeled data, finds patterns (clustering, dimensionality reduction). Reinforcement: agent learns by trial and error through rewards/penalties.

A large model pre-trained on vast, diverse data that serves as a base for many downstream tasks. Can be adapted via prompting, fine-tuning, or RAG. Examples: Gemini, GPT-4, BERT. Named for being the 'foundation' of many applications.

Dense numerical vector representations of data (text, images, audio) capturing semantic meaning. Similar items have vectors close together in high-dimensional space. Enable: semantic search, clustering, RAG retrieval, classification.

A generative model that learns to reverse a noise-addition process. Trained by: 1) Adding Gaussian noise step-by-step to training images. 2) Learning to reverse the denoising. At inference: starts from pure noise, iteratively denoises. Used in: Imagen, Stable Diffusion, DALL-E.

The maximum amount of text (measured in tokens) an LLM can process in a single interaction, including system prompt, conversation history, and documents. Gemini 1.5 Pro has 1M token context. Larger = can reason over more content at once.

Converting text into tokens — the units an LLM processes. Tokens are typically subwords (e.g., 'unhappy' → 'un' + 'happy'). Why it matters: LLM pricing is per token, context windows are measured in tokens, different languages tokenize differently.

AI that can process and generate multiple data types (modalities) — text, images, audio, video, and code. Gemini is natively multimodal. Enables tasks like: analyzing images with text questions, generating images from text descriptions, video understanding.

front: Name 5 key generative AI use cases
back: 1) Content creation (text, images, video), 2) Summarization (documents, meetings), 3) Code generation & debugging, 4) Conversational AI / chatbots, 5) Translation & language tasks. Also: data augmentation, search, personalization.

Automatically generating a concise, coherent summary of longer content. Two types: Extractive (selects key sentences from source) vs Abstractive (generates new summary text, like a human would). Gen AI excels at abstractive summarization.

When an LLM confidently generates factually incorrect, nonsensical, or fabricated information. Occurs because LLMs predict plausible text, not verified facts. Mitigation: RAG (grounding), fact-checking, human review, retrieval citations.

Systematic errors in AI causing unfair outcomes favoring some groups over others. Sources: biased training data reflecting historical inequalities, underrepresentation, biased labels. Impact: discriminatory outcomes in hiring, lending, healthcare, criminal justice.

1) Be socially beneficial, 2) Avoid creating/reinforcing unfair bias, 3) Be built and tested for safety, 4) Be accountable to people, 5) Incorporate privacy design principles, 6) Uphold high standards of scientific excellence, 7) Be made available for uses consistent with these principles.

The ability to understand and explain how an AI system makes decisions. Approaches: feature importance, attention visualization, LIME, SHAP. Why important: trust, accountability, debugging, regulatory compliance (e.g., GDPR right to explanation).

An attack where malicious instructions are embedded in user inputs or documents to hijack an LLM's behavior, overriding system instructions. Example: a document saying 'Ignore previous instructions and...' Mitigation: input validation, sandboxing, privilege separation.

Bilingual Evaluation Understudy. Measures n-gram overlap between generated and reference text. Range: 0-1 (higher=better). Originally for machine translation, used broadly for text generation. Limitation: doesn't capture semantic equivalence.

Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation. Measures overlap between generated and reference summaries. ROUGE-1 (unigrams), ROUGE-2 (bigrams), ROUGE-L (longest common subsequence). Commonly used for summarization evaluation.

front: What is perplexity in LLM evaluation?
back: Measures how well a language model predicts a test sample. Lower perplexity = better predictive performance. Formula: 2^(cross-entropy loss). Limitation: lower perplexity doesn't always mean better quality outputs for tasks.

front: Gemini model sizes and their use cases
back: Nano: on-device, low-latency tasks (mobile). Flash: fast, cost-efficient, high-volume production workloads. Pro: balanced capability and efficiency for enterprise tasks. Ultra: maximum capability for complex reasoning (research, advanced analysis).

Unlike models that add multimodal capabilities as extensions, Gemini was designed from the ground up to understand and reason across text, images, audio, video, and code simultaneously within a single unified model architecture.

Google Cloud's unified machine learning platform for the entire ML lifecycle: data preparation, model training, evaluation, deployment, and monitoring. Supports Gemini/foundation models, custom models, AutoML. Key tools: Workbench, Pipelines, Model Registry, Feature Store.

front: What is Vertex AI Model Monitoring?
back: A service that continuously monitors deployed ML models for: data drift (input distribution shift), prediction drift (output distribution shift), and feature attribution drift. Sends alerts when thresholds are exceeded, enabling proactive retraining.

front: What is Vertex AI Vector Search?
back: High-scale, low-latency approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) vector similarity search. Key use: retrieving semantically similar documents in RAG systems. Stores and indexes embeddings, enabling fast semantic search over billions of vectors.

Vertex AI's centralized catalog for discovering, testing, and deploying AI models. Includes: Google's Gemini models, open-source models (Llama, Mistral, Stable Diffusion), and specialized task models. Enables one-click deployment to endpoints.

A no-code interface within Vertex AI for prompt engineering and model testing. Features: multi-turn chat, text/image/code prompting, parameter adjustment (temperature, top-k/p, max tokens), prompt comparison, and API code export.

front: What is Gemini for Google Workspace?
back: AI features integrated into Google productivity tools: Gmail (draft/reply emails), Docs (write/edit content), Sheets (generate formulas, analyze data), Slides (create presentations), Meet (meeting notes, summaries), and Chat (AI assistant).

Google's AI-powered research assistant that grounds its responses in user-uploaded documents (PDFs, Docs, slides). Creates summaries, FAQs, briefing docs, and Audio Overviews. All responses are cited and traceable to source documents.

front: What is Google's Secure AI Framework (SAIF)?
back: Google's open framework for AI security with 6 core elements: 1) Expand strong security foundations to AI, 2) Extend detection/response for AI threats, 3) Automate defenses, 4) Harmonize platform-level controls, 5) Adapt controls for new AI risks, 6) Contextualize AI risk in existing processes.

A Google Cloud feature allowing organizations to use their own encryption keys (from Cloud KMS or external HSMs) to encrypt data at rest in GCP services. Provides data sovereignty, helps meet compliance requirements (HIPAA, GDPR, financial regulations).

front: What is Cloud Data Loss Prevention (DLP)?
back: Google Cloud service that discovers, classifies, and protects sensitive information (PII, credentials, financial data). For AI: ensures training data doesn't contain sensitive info, prevents PII from appearing in AI outputs. Uses 150+ built-in detectors.

Google Cloud's enterprise conversational AI platform using flow-based (state machine) design. Features: visual flow builder, intent classification, entity extraction, webhook integrations, multi-channel deployment (web, mobile, telephony). Suited for complex multi-turn conversations.

Google's enterprise AI platform giving employees an intelligent assistant that searches across business data sources and takes actions in enterprise systems. Powered by Gemini, it integrates with Google Workspace, ServiceNow, Salesforce, Jira, and other enterprise tools.

front: Zero-shot vs Few-shot vs Many-shot prompting
back: Zero-shot: no examples, model uses pre-trained knowledge. Few-shot: 1-5 examples in prompt to demonstrate format/behavior. Many-shot: tens to hundreds of examples. More examples generally improve performance but consume more tokens/context.

A prompting technique that encourages the model to show its reasoning step-by-step before giving a final answer. Improves: math problems, logical reasoning, multi-step tasks. Can be elicited by 'Let's think step by step' or demonstrated via few-shot examples with reasoning.

front: What is temperature in LLM generation?
back: Parameter controlling output randomness. Low (0.0-0.3): deterministic, focused, factual responses. Medium (0.4-0.7): balanced creativity and coherence. High (0.8-1.0+): creative, diverse, less predictable. Typical use: low for factual tasks, higher for creative writing.

Persistent instructions given to a model before the user conversation begins. Defines: model role/persona ('You are a helpful customer service agent'), output format, topic boundaries, language/tone, and behavioral constraints. Separates from user messages in API calls.

Samples from the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds p. Unlike top-k (fixed count), top-p adapts to probability distribution. Example: p=0.9 uses many tokens when probabilities spread, few when one token dominates. Good for natural, diverse text.

Architecture combining retrieval + generation: 1) Query → embedding, 2) Vector search finds relevant document chunks, 3) Retrieved context + query → LLM, 4) LLM generates grounded response with citations. Reduces hallucinations, keeps knowledge current without retraining.

front: What is grounding in generative AI?
back: Connecting model outputs to authoritative external information sources (documents, databases, search). Grounded models: cite their sources, stay factually anchored, reduce hallucinations, and can access current information beyond training cutoff. Key technique for enterprise AI reliability.

Search that understands meaning and intent rather than exact keyword matching. Works by: 1) Convert query to embedding, 2) Compare against document embeddings using cosine similarity, 3) Return most semantically similar results. Finds relevant content even without keyword overlap.

Splitting documents into smaller segments before embedding for retrieval. Strategies: fixed-size (N tokens), sentence-based, paragraph-based, semantic chunking. Key tradeoffs: too large → loses specificity; too small → loses context. Overlap between chunks helps maintain coherence.

front: What is fine-tuning a foundation model?
back: Further training a pre-trained foundation model on a smaller, task-specific dataset to improve performance on targeted tasks. Updates model weights. When to use: insufficient prompt engineering, need consistent style/format, domain-specific vocabulary, or behavior not achievable via prompting.

Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback. Process: 1) Generate model outputs, 2) Humans rank outputs, 3) Train reward model on rankings, 4) Fine-tune LLM using RL with reward model. Goal: align model behavior with human values (helpful, harmless, honest). Used in GPT-4, Gemini.

Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) technique: inserts small trainable adapter matrices into transformer layers, keeping original weights frozen. Benefits: 10-100x fewer trainable parameters, reduces VRAM needs, faster training, easy adapter swapping. Popular for fine-tuning LLMs cost-effectively.

front: What causes bias in AI systems?
back: Common sources: 1) Biased training data (historical inequalities), 2) Underrepresentation of groups, 3) Biased data collection methods, 4) Label bias (annotator subjectivity), 5) Model architecture choices, 6) Feedback loops amplifying initial biases. Bias can be in data, model, or deployment context.

front: What are safety filters in AI?
back: Content moderation systems applied to AI inputs/outputs that evaluate and block: hate speech, violence, sexual content, dangerous instructions, PII leakage. Can be: rule-based (keyword filters), model-based (classifier), or hybrid. Vertex AI Safety Filters allow threshold configuration.

front: What is an AI Center of Excellence (CoE)?
back: A centralized cross-functional team driving organizational AI adoption. Responsibilities: AI strategy and roadmap, standards and best practices, reusable platform components, business unit enablement, talent development, and governance. Prevents siloed, duplicated AI efforts.

front: Key challenges in AI change management
back: 1) Fear of job displacement (address through communication + upskilling), 2) Resistance from employees and middle management, 3) Skill gaps in AI literacy, 4) Data quality and access issues, 5) Culture of experimentation vs. risk aversion, 6) Executive sponsorship and alignment.

front: What is an AI Proof of Concept (PoC)?
back: A small-scale prototype validating whether AI can solve a specific business problem before full investment. PoC goals: technical feasibility, data quality validation, rough business value estimate, team learning. Principle: fail fast and cheaply to avoid large wasted investments.

front: How do you measure AI project success?
back: Business metrics: productivity gains (time saved), cost reduction, revenue uplift, customer satisfaction (NPS/CSAT), error rate reduction. Technical metrics (intermediate): model accuracy, latency, availability. Align metrics to business goals BEFORE implementation. ROI calculation is essential.

front: What is data governance for AI?
back: Policies, processes, and standards for managing data throughout the AI lifecycle: collection (consent, sourcing), quality (validation, labeling), security (access control, encryption), privacy (PII handling, anonymization), lineage (tracking origin/transformations), and compliance (GDPR, CCPA, HIPAA).

AI system design pattern where humans review, validate, or override AI decisions at critical points. When to use: high-stakes decisions, low-confidence predictions, novel situations, regulatory requirements. Balances automation efficiency with human judgment and accountability.

Being open about how AI systems work: training data sources, model architecture decisions, known limitations and failure modes, intended and prohibited use cases, performance metrics across demographic groups. Builds trust and enables appropriate human oversight.

Google's SynthID embeds invisible watermarks in AI-generated content (images, text, audio) detectable by Google's system. Purpose: identify AI-generated content, enable content attribution, combat misinformation/deepfakes. Watermark is imperceptible to humans but detectable by the system.

front: Gen AI use cases in Healthcare
back: 1) Clinical note summarization, 2) Medical record extraction/structuring, 3) Research paper summarization, 4) Drug discovery support, 5) Patient communication drafting, 6) Prior authorization assistance, 7) Medical coding automation. Always with physician oversight and strict compliance.

front: Gen AI use cases in Financial Services
back: 1) Intelligent document processing (contracts, filings), 2) Fraud pattern explanation, 3) Personalized financial advice summaries, 4) Investment research summarization, 5) Risk report generation, 6) Customer service chatbots, 7) Regulatory compliance document analysis.

front: Gen AI use cases in Retail
back: 1) Personalized product recommendations, 2) Automated product descriptions, 3) AI shopping assistants, 4) Personalized email/ad copy generation, 5) Customer service chatbots, 6) Inventory and demand forecasting narrative, 7) Visual search and virtual try-ons.

front: Gen AI use cases in Manufacturing
back: 1) Predictive maintenance reports and anomaly explanation, 2) Technical documentation generation, 3) Supply chain scenario analysis, 4) Quality control report automation, 5) Engineering assistant for technical queries, 6) Safety procedure generation, 7) Training material creation.

front: Structured vs Unstructured data — definitions and AI relevance
back: Structured: predefined schema, rows/columns (databases, spreadsheets, CSV). Unstructured: no fixed format (~80% of enterprise data) — text, email, images, audio, video. Gen AI excels at extracting value from unstructured data; traditional ML prefers structured data.

front: 6 dimensions of data quality for AI
back:
1) Completeness — all required records/fields present
2) Consistency — same concept represented uniformly
3) Relevance — data applies to the task at hand
4) Availability — data is accessible when needed
5) Cost — affordable to collect/store/process
6) Format — data is in a usable structure for the model

Labeled: human-annotated input-output pairs. Used for supervised learning (classification, regression). Expensive to create. Unlabeled: raw data with no annotations. Used for unsupervised learning and self-supervised pre-training (foundation models). Cheap to collect at scale.

front: The 5 layers of the gen AI landscape
back:
Bottom to top:
1) Infrastructure — compute (TPUs, GPUs, hypercomputers, data centres)
2) Models — foundation models (Gemini, Gemma, Imagen, Veo)
3) Platforms — tools to build/manage AI (Vertex AI, Model Garden)
4) Agents — AI systems that reason and act using models + tools
5) Applications — end-user products (Workspace, Gemini app, CCaaS)

front: Google's 4 foundation models: Gemini, Gemma, Imagen, Veo
back:
Gemini: natively multimodal LLM (text, image, audio, video, code). Closed, API-accessed.
Gemma: lightweight open-weight models. Self-hostable, fine-tunable, on-device capable.
Imagen: text-to-image diffusion model. High-quality image generation.
Veo: text-to-video generation model. Creates video clips from prompts.

Choose based on: Modality (what input/output types needed?), Context window (how long is the input?), Security & Privacy (data residency, CMEK?), Availability & Reliability (SLA requirements?), Cost (per-token pricing), Performance (benchmark scores for your task), Fine-tuning (can it be customised?), Customisation (open vs. closed weights).

front: What are Gems in Gemini Advanced?
back: Custom AI personas/assistants within Gemini Advanced. Users configure a Gem with specific instructions, knowledge, and tone for a recurring task (e.g., 'Marketing Copywriter Gem'). Saves repeated prompting. Available to Gemini Advanced subscribers — Google's premium AI product.

Extends Gemini for Workspace with: NotebookLM Enterprise API (grounded research at scale), advanced multimodal search across enterprise content, custom agent capabilities for complex workflows, enhanced security and compliance controls, and higher usage limits — for large enterprise deployments.

front: Google Customer Engagement Suite — 4 key components
back:
1) Conversational Agents — AI chatbots for customer self-service
2) Agent Assist — real-time AI support for human agents (suggestions, knowledge retrieval)
3) Conversational Insights — AI analytics of call/chat transcripts (trends, sentiment, compliance)
4) Contact Center as a Service (CCaaS) — cloud contact centre platform with embedded AI

Enterprise search platform powered by gen AI. Searches across an organisation's documents, databases, and websites. Returns AI-synthesised answers with citations (not just document links). Use cases: internal knowledge search, customer-facing product search, regulatory document retrieval.

front: Gen AI agent tooling: Extensions, Functions, Data Stores, Plugins
back:
Extensions: connect agent to external APIs/services in real time (call weather API, submit forms).
Functions: run custom code/logic to process data within an agent workflow.
Data Stores: structured/unstructured knowledge the agent can search (RAG knowledge base).
Plugins: pre-packaged integrations with third-party services (e.g., Slack, Salesforce).

front: Vertex AI Studio vs Google AI Studio
back:
Google AI Studio (ai.google.dev): free, browser-based, developer-focused prototyping tool. Quick experiments, API key access, no GCP account needed.
Vertex AI Studio: enterprise GCP service. Production-grade. Full IAM, VPC, security, billing, MLOps integration. For building production AI applications.

ReAct = Reasoning + Acting. Pattern: Thought (reason about next step) → Action (call tool/API) → Observation (process result) → Thought → ... → Answer. Enables LLMs to act as autonomous agents that complete multi-step tasks by interleaving reasoning with real-world actions. Key technique for agentic AI systems.

Decomposing a complex task into a sequence of simpler prompts where each output feeds into the next input. Benefits: simpler steps are more reliable, enables specialised models per step, allows error checking between steps, produces structured outputs. Example: Extract → Summarise → Translate → Format as JSON.

Anonymisation: irreversibly remove all identifiers. Individual CANNOT be re-identified. Fewer regulatory restrictions. Use when sharing data externally.
Pseudonymisation: replace identifiers with tokens. Re-identification POSSIBLE with mapping key. Key kept secure. Use when you need to share data but retain ability to re-link results. Both governed by GDPR/privacy laws.

Centralised security and risk management platform for GCP. For AI workloads: continuous vulnerability scanning, threat detection across Vertex AI resources, misconfiguration alerts, compliance reporting. Gives security teams unified visibility into AI infrastructure security posture across the full ML lifecycle.
